fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this HTTP-to-backend transcoding layer we've been talking about. Basically the idea is: clients send normal HTTP requests with JSON bodies, and we need to route them to the right handler and convert everything into our internal message format — and do the reverse for responses. Think of it like that API gateway translation work we did on the billing service, but generalized.

The big pain points we're hearing from developers: they keep writing the same URL parsing and JSON mapping glue code over and over, it's inconsistent across services, and whenever someone adds a new endpoint they have to touch like five different files. We want one declarative place to define routes and schemas.

The system needs to handle path patterns with variables (including nested ones), action-style suffixes at the end of paths (you know, the colon thing), query parameter extraction, and both single-message and streaming flows in both directions. There's also some nuance around how percent-encoded characters in URLs get decoded — single-segment vs multi-segment captures behave differently, which has been a bug source.

For the message format side, field names come in camelCase from JSON but need to map to the snake_case internal names. Unknown fields should just be dropped. We also need to support placing a JSON body under a specific sub-field rather than at the top level.

More detailed specs are in the usual place. Please make this production-grade, not a quick hack.

trigger_keywords: what makes a path pattern invalid
when should parse_template return valid=false
which patterns are rejected by the parser
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A path pattern is invalid if it does not start with '/', contains empty path segments (e.g. double slashes), or attaches a variable expression inside a literal segment (e.g. '/a{x}' is invalid because '{x}' is embedded in the literal 'a'). All other patterns including wildcards, double-star wildcards, named variables with sub-patterns, and optional colon suffixes are valid.

trigger_keywords: how are variables described in the segments output
what does the segments line look like for variable patterns
how do {field=literal/*} patterns appear in segments
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the 'segments=' output line, each variable is expanded into its matching pattern rather than shown as '{field}'. A plain '{field}' or '{field=*}' becomes '*', a '{field=literal/*}' expands to its literal segments followed by '*', and '{field=**}' or '{field=literal/**}' expands to the equivalent wildcard segments. The variable name itself does not appear in the segments line.

trigger_keywords: how is the variables line formatted
what does the index notation mean in variable output
what is the single|multi indicator in variable output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each variable is reported as 'field.path[start:end:single|multi]' where start and end are zero-based segment indices (start inclusive, end exclusive, counting from the first segment after the leading slash), and 'single' means the variable captures exactly one segment while 'multi' means it captures multiple segments (i.e., uses '**'). Multiple variables are separated by semicolons.

trigger_keywords: how does route specificity work
which route wins when multiple patterns match
how are exact routes preferred over wildcard routes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When multiple routes match a request, the most specific route wins. An exact literal segment beats a single-segment wildcard (*), which beats a multi-segment wildcard (**). Routes with more literal segments rank higher. The route_index returned is the zero-based index of the winning route in the original routes array.

trigger_keywords: how does percent decoding differ between single and multi segment captures
what characters are preserved in multi-segment variable decoding
how are encoded slashes handled in path variable extraction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Single-segment captures (using '*') decode all percent-escaped characters including encoded slashes. Multi-segment captures (using '**') decode only unreserved characters by default, preserving reserved characters such as encoded slashes (%2F) in their percent-encoded form. This means '%2F' in a multi-segment capture stays as '%2F' unless a broader unescape mode is explicitly requested.

trigger_keywords: how do system parameters work
what happens to api_key and key query parameters
how are configured system params excluded from bindings
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Query parameters listed in the route's 'system_params' array are excluded from the extracted bindings. All other query parameters are included as bindings after path variable bindings. Query parameter values are fully percent-decoded regardless of their content.

trigger_keywords: how does the colon verb suffix matching work
when is a colon in a path treated as an action suffix vs literal text
what happens when the colon suffix is not configured in any route
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A colon suffix at the end of the final path segment is treated as a terminal action selector only if that exact suffix text appears in at least one registered route pattern. If no registered route has that colon suffix, the colon and following text are treated as normal literal path content. A colon that appears before the final slash (i.e., not at the end of the last segment) is always treated as normal path content.

trigger_keywords: how are camelCase JSON fields mapped to schema fields
what happens to unknown fields in the JSON body
how does lowerCamel to snake_case conversion work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Lower-camel JSON field names are mapped to their corresponding snake_case structured field names (e.g., 'authorInfo' maps to 'author_info', 'firstName' maps to 'first_name'). Unknown JSON fields that have no corresponding field in the schema are silently ignored and do not cause an error.

trigger_keywords: how does body_target work
what happens when a body target points to a nested field
how are URL bindings merged with the body when body_target is set
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When 'body_target' is set to a field name, the entire JSON body is placed under that field in the output message rather than at the top level. URL-derived bindings are then merged into the resulting message after body placement. If a binding path conflicts with a field already set from the body, the binding value is applied (bindings overlay the body).

trigger_keywords: what is the text format for output messages
how are nested message fields printed in the output
how is the message text representation formatted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Messages are printed in a stable proto-text-style format: scalar fields as 'field_name: "value"' (strings quoted), nested message fields as 'field_name {\n  ...\n}' with two-space indentation per nesting level. Fields within a message are sorted alphabetically by field name. Each message block is terminated by '--end--' on its own line.

trigger_keywords: what does always_print_primitive_fields do
how are default empty values handled in proto_to_json output
when are zero or empty string fields included in JSON output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, fields with default/zero values (empty string, 0, false) are omitted from the JSON output. When 'always_print_primitive_fields' is set to true, all primitive fields defined in the schema are included in the JSON output even if they hold their default value (e.g., empty string fields appear as '"fieldName":""').

trigger_keywords: how does streaming mode work for proto_to_json
how are multiple response messages output in streaming mode
what is the format of the streaming JSON response array
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In streaming proto_to_json mode, all response messages are collected and output as a single JSON array on one line, e.g. 'json=[{...},{...}]'. In non-streaming mode a single message is output as a plain JSON object, e.g. 'json={...}'. Frame errors such as 'incomplete_frame_header' are reported as 'error=<category>' and terminate processing.

trigger_keywords: what schemas are available
what message types does the system support
what fields does the shelf or book schema have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system uses a fixed set of built-in schemas for testing. 'shelf' has string fields 'name' and 'theme'. 'book' has string fields 'name', 'author', 'title', and a nested message field 'author_info' containing string fields 'first_name' and 'last_name'. 'create_book_request' has a nested 'book' field of type 'book'. These schemas drive both json_to_proto and proto_to_json translation.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that API gateway translation work we did on the billing service
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The HTTP/JSON transcoding architecture described in start.md, specifically the route matching (Features 2-4) and payload translation (Features 5-8) subsystems that together form the transcoding pipeline

Terminal action suffix matching as defined in Feature 4: a colon-separated suffix at the end of the final path segment (e.g. ':verb') that acts as a route discriminator only when configured in a registered route pattern
start.md (the PRD) and the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/, specifically feature1 through feature8 JSON test files

context_pointers.vague_reference: single-segment vs multi-segment captures behave differently
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The percent-decoding policy described in Feature 3: single-segment '{field}' or '{field=*}' captures fully decode all percent-escaped characters, while multi-segment '{field=**}' captures preserve reserved characters like encoded slashes by default

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now. Could you check the start.md spec and the relevant test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — those should have the authoritative behavior. If something still seems ambiguous after reviewing those, let me know what you found and we can figure it out together.

feature1_template_parsing.json.description: Parse HTTP-style path patterns into matchable segments, variables, and optional terminal action suffixes; malformed patterns are rejected.

feature2_route_matching.json.description: Select the registered route whose HTTP method and path pattern fully match the request, preferring exact and more specific patterns over wildcards and rejecting partial matches.

feature3_variable_binding.json.description: Extract named values from matched path variables and query parameters, decoding percent-escaped values according to whether the capture spans one path segment or multiple segments.

feature4_terminal_action_matching.json.description: Treat a configured colon suffix at the end of a path as a terminal action segment, while leaving unconfigured colon text as normal path content.

feature5_json_request_translation.json.description: Translate JSON request bodies into typed request messages, optionally placing body content under a configured field and merging URL-derived bindings.

feature6_streaming_json_request_translat.description: Translate a JSON array input as a stream of request messages, including scalar and repeated-field body targets, and reject non-array input in streaming mode.

cases.expected_output:
message[0]=
name: "1"
theme: "Russian"
--end--
message[1]=
name: "2"
theme: "History"
--end--
message[2]=
name: "3"
theme: "Mistery"
--end--
finished=true

feature7_proto_response_translation.json.description: Translate framed binary response messages into JSON objects, preserving nested data and optionally printing primitive default values.

feature8_streaming_proto_response_transl.description: Translate a stream of framed binary response messages into one JSON array and report malformed frame errors using neutral error categories.
